does: This paper presents Mini-Unlearning, a machine unlearning method that removes private data from trained models while preserving model utility and privacy guarantees. The core insight is that unlearned parameters exhibit a contraction mapping relationship with retraining parameters, allowing the method to operate with only a minimal subset of historical gradients rather than storing comprehensive gradient histories. The approach is evaluated on its ability to maintain accuracy across various unlearning ratios while defending against membership inference attacks, demonstrating improvements over existing gradient-based unlearning techniques in both computational efficiency and model robustness.
connects: This work builds on the established line of machine unlearning research that emerged to address the challenge of making trained models forget specific training examples or classes. It extends prior gradient-based unlearning methods that typically require storing extensive historical gradient information, responding to the computational and memory inefficiency of those approaches. The paper engages with the dual-objective framework common in unlearning literature: maintaining model performance while guaranteeing privacy protection against membership inference attacks. By introducing the contraction mapping principle, it combines insights from optimization theory with practical unlearning, advancing beyond simple retraining-based and influence-function-based approaches that preceded it.
enables: The minimal gradient dependence of Mini-Unlearning opens paths toward scalable unlearning in large-scale models and real-world deployment scenarios where storing historical gradients is prohibitively expensive. This work suggests that unlearning can operate efficiently at high unlearning ratios relevant to practical applications like right-to-be-forgotten requests at scale. Future work could explore applying the contraction mapping principle to other privacy-preserving learning paradigms, extending the method to federated learning settings where gradient communication is bottlenecked, or investigating theoretical guarantees on the privacy-utility tradeoff. The lightweight nature of this approach may enable unlearning as a post-training service for already-deployed models.
